data.title: Ardelyx: Strong IBSRELA Growth, But Heavy Operational Spend Clouds Profitability
data.description:
Thesis: Have to justify a hefty operational spend

Ardelyx, Inc. (ARDX) just reported fourth quarter results and actually beat on revenue, but they missed slightly on earnings, which the market took negatively, as you may know. Q4 2025 revenue came in at $125.25 million, up about 7.9% year over year, a figure that beat estimates by $7.21 million. Revenue here was mainly driven by strong Ibsrela sales. As for earnings, the company’s GAAP EPS of $0.00 missed by $0.01 due to some sharply higher costs, including SG&A, which jumped to $337.2 million, and R&D, to $71.5 million for the year.

Now, management has put this down to the need for scaling commercialization and pipeline programs. FY 2025 showed strong Ibsrela growth but a pretty steep decline in Xphozah revenue, with a net loss widening to $61.6 million. This is one area where the market would have some concerns that possibly expenses are rising faster than profitability despite top-line strength. We also need to take a deep look at what's expected from 2026 with such a big research spend. Ardelyx has the Phase 3 CIC trial and their next-generation NHE3 inhibitor program taking up a large portion of the spend, so it's worth trying to value the longer-term potential in these areas.

FY 2025 earnings

Ardelyx has delivered a pretty notable revenue performance for FY 2025, and the initial market reaction is quite negative. Shares find themselves trading about 8% lower in pre-market today. As for company performance. We saw explosive growth in Ibsrela, which was much needed to offset the hefty contraction in Xphozah. However, I think there was still decent top-line expansion overall. Total revenue jumped to $407.3 million, up from $333.6 million in 2024, which was mainly driven by Ibsrela’s surge to $274.2 million, representing 73% year-over-year growth. Ibsrela now makes up about 82% of the company's revenue. The big chunk came from Q4 2025, where Ibsrela alone contributed $86.6 million, up 61% versus Q4 2024 and about 11% sequentially.

So, that tells us there’s not just annual growth here but accelerating quarterly momentum for that asset. Management mentioned on the call that this is due to deeper penetration among target prescribers and just all-around improved patient engagement in IBS-C. It’s led to much better prescription pull-through, which management is seeing in both volume and adherence improvements.

More importantly is that we see this same momentum in 2026, and on that front, management is guiding for Ibsrela to bring in revenue of $410 to $430 million, which would mean another 50% plus growth year. They’re also projecting for a hefty $1 billion in annual revenue by 2029, which is impressive. Again, Ibsrela is expected to make up the larger chunk of that target and should be regarded as the company’s primary long-term value driver. Keep in mind that this guidance is paired with patent protection through 2042, which, in my opinion, substantially de-risks the franchise from a competitive standpoint.

So on the flip side, Xphozah revenue is taking big hits. Revenue in this area declined to $103.6 million from $160.9 million in 2024, despite Q4 2025 revenue reaching $27.8 million and reported growth in total dispenses. This tells me that pricing, payer mix, or reimbursement headwinds are playing a negative role here, particularly with regard to Medicare populations. There’s clearly a good deal of pressure on recognized revenue even if utilization is expanding. Management’s expectation for 2026 is for revenue of $110 to $120 million, which would just reflect modest growth. So it would seem Xphozah is transitioning into a more mature, slower-growth asset compared to Ibsrela. This was somewhat expected.

Elsewhere on the cost side, Ardelyx increased R&D spending, now up to $71.5 million, about a 37% increase compared to last year, and we can put this down mainly to the launch of the Phase 3 CIC trial (ACCEL) and their next-generation NHE3 inhibitor program (RDX10531). There’s a big strategic reinvestment of commercial cash flows into pipeline expansion here, and I think we can expect much of the potential upside in 2026 to rest in this area, as I'll explain. SG&A also rose pretty sharply, now up to $337.2 million, about 30% up year-over-year. In my opinion, the SG&A cost is massive. To put it in context, it's even higher than FY24 revenue, which is concerning. This would be due to the operational reality of scaling two commercial products, but even so, it is still rather large. There’s a heavy commercialization spend, and it would be the primary reason the company posted a larger net loss of $61.6 million, despite higher revenue.

As for cash, the company holds $264.7 million in cash and short-term investments and is set to grow operating leverage from Ibsrela. The balance sheet appears quite sufficient to fund those near-term trials, but not with the current operational spend. If we see an R&D expense as we did in 2025, then that cash figure would only last about 11 quarters, and that's without including SG&A.

Now, according to the company’s most recent SEC filings, Ardelyx filed an automatic shelf registration back in November last year. So it can offer up to $100 million of common stock, preferred stock, debt securities, warrants, or units over time. So they have the flexibility to raise capital via equity if needed, but it just doesn't seem justified to do so, given what they're making in sales.

Phase 3 ACCEL Trial: Expanding tenapanor’s reach beyond IBS-C

As you know, one of Ardelyx’s key commercial products going forward is tenapanor, which is marketed as Ibsrela in the U.S. The drug essentially works by inhibiting the sodium/hydrogen exchanger isoform 3 (NHE3) in the intestine. Now, we typically see NHE3 as a transporter on the surface of gut cells responsible for absorbing dietary sodium. So in blocking this transporter, tenapanor can increase sodium, and therefore water, in the intestinal lumen.

The benefits of this are very important; patients see softening of stool and accelerated transit, which helps relieve constipation and other abdominal symptoms seen in IBS. However, it also seems to improve visceral sensitivity and reduce intestinal permeability. This effect can help with pain and discomfort associated with bowel dysfunction.

So, with IBS-C already addressed by current approval, what Ardelyx is looking to achieve with the Phase 3 ACCEL trial is to demonstrate that tenapanor can also be effective for chronic idiopathic constipation (CIC). Keep in mind that this is a separate but related disorder that affects tens of millions of adults. It’s often characterized by infrequent or difficult bowel movements and, of course, a markedly reduced quality of life. The ACCEL trial is a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study that’s tipped to enroll about 700 adult patients. The first patient got dosed last month with tenapanor, being taken twice daily over 26 weeks. The primary endpoint for the trial is based on patient-reported outcomes in constipation. So that means it’s designed to specifically show that tenapanor provides meaningful relief from the daily symptoms. This is what matters most to patients for a more improved quality of life.

Now, from a business perspective, CIC would mean a much larger potential market than IBS-C alone. So success in ACCEL could allow Ardelyx to then go on and file a Supplemental New Drug Application with the FDA to expand the label. The end goal being broadening tenapanor’s commercial reach and significantly increasing that long-term revenue potential we’re already seeing from Ibsrela. The downside is that Phase 3 trials are inherently expensive, especially with such a large enrollment number.

So a good deal of upside for the stock would be in Ardelyx succeeding in expanding Ibsrela into CIC. As for the new TAM, the new market would become quite large. You see, CIC affects an estimated 34 million adults in the U.S. (as per the company's press release), and with Ibsrela’s cash list price at about $2,000 to $2,500 per month, that translates to about $24,000 to $30,000 per year. However, we should keep in mind that the net realized pricing is a bit lower due to rebates and assistance programs. So a reasonable modelling assumption is for $20,000 to $25,000 per treated patient per year. So if we see Ibsrela for CIC priced similarly to IBS-C, which is highly likely given identical molecule and dosing, and we also see decent adoption that mirrors Ibsrela’s early IBS-C penetration, the upside could be quite substantial. It would be in the order of about 5% of the target population over time, so based on the CIC U.S. instances, that would imply roughly 1.7 million CIC patients on therapy.

Now, at $22,000 per patient per year, this equates to roughly $37 billion in theory. However, this is only a highly theoretical estimate, and in reality, adoption will be far slower. You see, the TAM for CIC is so large because constipation without the IBS pain component is far more prevalent than IBS-C. Chronic idiopathic constipation affects about 32 million adults in the U.S., versus an estimated 2.4 to 3.5 million patient visits with IBS-C. However, an annual TAM, even at a more conservative scenario of 1% penetration, so 340,000 patients, would still represent about $7.5 billion in annual revenue potential. This is just to show you why Ardelyx may be willing to absorb the high cost of a Phase 3 trial to pursue this label expansion. But keep in mind, this is a very loose model based on the list price of Ibsrela, and in reality that price may differ between patients.

We can dig into the reported 2025 Ibsrela revenue of $274.2 million a bit more. The estimated net price of roughly $20,000 to $25,000 per patient per year would give us about 11,000 to 14,000 patients who are currently on Ibsrela for IBS-C. That means penetration of the IBS-C population today is still well under 1%, showing us just how early the commercial curve still is.

Valuation

When it comes to a valuation, the selloff today has made some metrics screen a bit more attractively, but Ardelyx is still trading as a revenue-scaled commercial biotech rather than a pre-revenue story. The most relevant metrics would be sales-based multiples and forward earnings power. They hold a market cap of about $1.64 billion and an enterprise value of $1.63 billion. Ardelyx trades at about 4.1x trailing EV to sales on $407 million of 2025 revenue and, more importantly, 2.9x forward EV to sales, which is slightly below the healthcare sector median and also dramatically lower than its own five-year average near 15x. So there’s obviously still a lot of investor skepticism around profitability and expense growth, especially when the label expansion of Ibsrela, their new largest growth driver, is far from confirmed. Consensus models for forward GAAP price to earnings show sharp operating leverage emerging, compressing to 11x in 2027 and about 6.6x in 2028.

This setup tells me the stock is being valued mainly on future margin expansion, not current profits, with the market assigning only modest multiple expansion due to the risks of that hefty operational spending. Despite this, they do hold a revenue base growing 50%+ annually. So in short, Ardelyx could be viewed as pretty cheap relative to its historical sales multiple but rather expensive relative to the current earnings figure they just posted. It makes the stock highly sensitive to execution on more Ibsrela growth and cost discipline.

Looking ahead

Long story short, that operational spend seems way too high. It's tipped to be driven by the commercialization of Ibsrela and Xphzoah, plus R&D for the Phase 3 ACCEL trial and next-generation NHE3 program. There's a lot going on; we can give the company that, but we have to think if this spend is strategically justifiable given the potential upside from expanding tenapanor into CIC. Also, Xphzoah sales are leveling out, so it would be better viewing if cost-saving measures were also put in place to offset those areas instead of solely relying on the jump in Ibsrela sales.

So, for me, the company will need to demonstrate better cost management over time for investors to build confidence here. In the near term, expect some stock price turbulence, since revenue growth is strong but margins remain under pressure. Investors are likely best to wait before buying in, especially with the topline Phase 3 ACCEL results expected sometime in 2H27. Successful CIC data could meaningfully de-risk the growth story, but between then and now, I would give this company a Hold rating based on what we just saw on earnings.

data.title: Public Storage: Strong REIT With An A2 Credit Profile, But The Opportunity Is In The Preferreds
data.description:
Public Storage (PSA) remains one of the highest-quality and financially sound REITs. The company has a strong balance sheet, high asset quality, and an investment-grade credit rating. However, at current market valuations, the common stock appears to be more fairly valued, and the more attractive option at present is the preferred stock, which offers current yields exceeding 6% with similar investment-grade credit risk. If you want to see our last article for the company, you can check it out here.

PSA in details

Public Storage (PSA) is one of the largest REIT companies and owners of self-storage facilities. Its current market capitalization is close to $52.85 billion, and the total assets of the last 4th quarter were close to $20.208 billion. The total debt of the company is around $10.53 billion, and the EBITDA is ~ $3.406 billion. PSA has more than 3400 properties across 40 states and close to 243 million rentable square feet. The company has a credit rating of "A2" from Moody's and "A" from S&P.

In the fourth quarter of 2025, PSA reported core FFO of $4.26 per share for the quarter and core FFO for the full year of $16.97 per share. Same-store sales revenue decreased 0.2% year-over-year. Management outlined its PS4.0 initiative, a strategic transformation focused on modernizing operations and improving technology integration. The company also detailed changes to its management and board to support the next phase of growth. These updates can be considered a shift from purely scale-oriented growth to operational optimization and digital efficiency. For 2026, PSA forecasts core FFO of $16.35–$17.00 per share, suggesting potential for future growth. You can see more details here. In our view, PSA is moving into a more normalized environment, but its structural strengths remain intact. The outlook looks stable with potential for future growth.

We will calculate some important financial metrics for the company, using the new information from Q4. The asset yield of PSA ((total revenue - operating expenses + depreciation and amortization) / total assets) is close to 16.86%. The Asset Coverage Ratio (total assets/debt) is ~ 192%. Operating expenses from the total revenue (excluding depreciation and amortization) are around 29.52%. The interest expenses from the total debt are close to 2.89%. We also calculate the market-adjusted asset yield to be more accurate. Therefore, we will need a price-to-book value, which is, at the time of writing, 10.76. We replace the book value of equity with its market value by applying the current p/b. From here, we can calculate the market-adjusted asset yield of around 3.06%, and the market-adjusted asset coverage ratio is the highest in the sector, close to the extremely 1000%. The forward price to AFFO is close to 18.91, so the expected AFFO yield for the company is close to 5.29%. Combined with moderate future growth guidance, this supports that the common stock is reasonably priced. You can see the basic valuation metrics below:

Credibility

We know that PSA has an "A2" credit rating by Moody's, but we want to show you the separate credit metrics of its rating, using Moody's methodology again:

The gross assets of PSA are close to $31.6 billion (the total assets+accumulated depreciation), or equivalent to the highest rating of an "Aaa". The same is with the unencumbered assets (most of the assets are unencumbered) and the secured debt, which is less than 1% - those two metrics are equivalent to an "Aaa" credit rating too. The total debt plus preferred stock as a percentage of the gross assets is close to 46%, which is equivalent to a "Baa3" credit rating. The ratio of the net debt to EBITDA is close to 7.9, which is equivalent to a "Baa1." EBITDA covers interest and preferred stock dividends at a rate of nearly five times, and for fixed charge coverage, the credit rating is equivalent to an "A1." The market positioning, asset quality, and operating environment of PSA receive an "A2" credit rating. And for liquidity and access to capital: "A1."

The overall credit score is an "A2," just like Moody's rates the company, as we mentioned above. But after calculating all ratios based on market-adjusted characteristics, we obtain an "A1" credit rating. But this should be interpreted with caution. Market adjustments can overstate the strength of assets during periods of elevated stock valuations.

Moody's data confirms PSA's structural soundness. The company benefits from scale, high-quality real estate, minimal secured debt, and ongoing fixed-charge coverage.

Below are shown all credit metrics:

Preferred Stocks

PSA's preferred stocks are rated with "BBB+" by S&P and an "A3" by Moody's. Their current yield range is from 5.92%-6.20%. All of them traded below par at the time of writing. Most of the stocks are callable already, except Public Storage 4.100% DP PFD S (PSA.PR.S), Public Storage 3.950% DEP PFD Q (PSA.PR.Q), Public Storage 4% DEP SHS PFD P (PSA.PR.P), and Public Storage 4% DEP PFD SHS R (PSA.PR.R), which have less than a year to call. Below are shown the potential prices and current yield of the rest.

Public Storage 3.875% DEP PFD N (PSA.PR.N): coupon rate 3.88%, annual amount $0.96875, and current yield 6.20%:

Public Storage 4.125 DP SH PF M (PSA.PR.M): coupon rate 4.13%, annual amount $1.03125, and current yield 6.19%:

Public Storage 4.7% DP RP PFD J (PSA.PR.J): coupon rate 4.10%, annual amount $1.18, and current yield 6.19%:

Public Storage 4.75 DP PFD SH K (PSA.PR.K): coupon rate 4.75%, annual amount $1.19, and current yield 6.14%:

Public Storage 3.9% DEP SHS REP 1/1000 PFD BEN INT SR O (PSA.PR.O): coupon rate 3.90%, annual amount $0.975, and current yield 6.13%:

Public Storage 4.875% DP SH PFD (PSA.PR.I): coupon rate 4.88%, annual amount $1.22, and current yield 6.12%:

Public Storage 4.625 DEP PFD L (PSA.PR.L): coupon rate 4.63%, annual amount $1.15625, and current yield 6.11%:

Public Storage PFD SHS F 5.15% (PSA.PR.F): coupon rate 5.15%, annual amount $1.29, and current yield 6.08%:

Public Storage 5.05% CUM PFD G (PSA.PR.G): coupon rate 5.05%, annual amount $1.26, and current yield 5.98%:

Public Storage 5.60 DEPSHS RP H (PSA.PR.H): coupon rate 5.60%, annual amount $1.40, and current yield 5.92%:

OTC Bonds

Below are shown the over-the-counter bonds of PSA:

All are rated with an "A2" credit rating by Moody's. The highest yield from the group is close to 5.45% yield to maturity, with around 0.8% spread to the treasury yield.

In the next chart, we see all the REIT Industrial sector OTC bonds. The highest in the group is only close to 5.51%. PSA's preferred stocks are higher, with close to 0.70%; the stocks are more attractive, especially when they are with a similar investment-grade category credit rating. Additionally, preferred shares have preferential tax treatment for some investors, and the difference in after-tax returns may be even more enticing.

Conclusion

PSA is a stable REIT with a high-quality portfolio, strong market positioning, and an investment-grade credit rating. The preferred stocks offer a yield of around and above 6% (close to 1% more than the sector's bonds). The overall credit picture supports the official A2 rating, but market-adjusted metrics likely overstate the true credit strength. PSA is suitable for long-term conservative investors, but at current levels, we prefer preferred stocks, which offer current yields above 6% with similar credit risk.

data.title: BNY Mellon AMT-Free Municipal Bond Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
“We believe municipal credit conditions should continue to remain stable.”

Market Review

The municipal bond market extended its rally in the fourth quarter, outperforming U.S. Treasuries and corporate bonds, supported by relatively favorable technicals and two additional 25 bp Fed rate cuts. Despite two consecutive quarters of strength, municipal bonds trailed other fixed income sectors for the full year. On the supply side, new issuance accelerated in November and December after a soft October, bringing total 4Q25 issuance to $141 billion, up 12.5% from 4Q24, according to the Bond Buyer. Full-year supply set another new annual record of $580 billion, up 12.9% year over year, driven in part by front-running tax policy uncertainties, higher cost of capital expenditure due to higher inflation, and waning of pandemic-related funds. Taxable municipal issuance remained low at $33 billion in 2025, down 12.4% year over year. In 4Q25, investor demand remained robust and municipal funds saw strong inflows, especially into municipal ETFs. Per Investment Company Institute data, municipal open-ended mutual funds had net inflows of $13 billion, while ETFs gained $45 billion for the whole year 2025.

Performance Summary

For the quarter ended December 31, 2025, the Fund’s Class I shares (DMBIX) returned 1.66%, excluding sales charges. In comparison, the Fund’s unmanaged benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Municipal Bond Index, returned 1.56% for the same period.

The Net Expenses is the actual fund expense ratio applicable to investors. The net expense ratios reflect a contractual expense reduction agreement through 12/31/2025, without which the performance would have been lower. Not all classes of shares may be available to all investors or through all broker-dealer platforms.

Market Review (continued)

During the fourth quarter, the municipal yield curve twisted flatter, with yields from two- to five-year maturities increasing 8 to 19 bps, while yields from ten to thirty years decreased 8 to 27 bps. To the contrary, the treasury yield curve twisted steeper, with yields from one-to-three-year maturities decreasing 1 to 14 bps, while yields from five-to-thirty years increased 5 to 14 bps. As a result, the Muni-Treasury ratio, a key gauge of relative value, cheapened in the short 2-5-year maturity range while richening in the 10-30-year part. The 2-year AAA Muni-to- Treasury ratio increased to 70% from 62%, while the 10-year ratio decreased to 65% from 70% and the 30Y ratio decreased to 85% from 89%, which were at richer side of fair value. In 4Q25, shorter duration municipal bonds underperformed their intermediate and longer counterparts, reflected in the Bloomberg Municipal 3-year Index return of 0.46%, compared to the Bloomberg Municipal 10-year and long Index returns of 1.74% and 1.63% respectively.

From a credit rating perspective during 4Q25, the lowest investment grade segment delivered relatively stronger performance, reflected by the Bloomberg Municipal BBB Index return of 1.77%, outperforming the Bloomberg Municipal AAA, AA & A Index returns of 1.57%, 1.57%, and 1.50% respectively. Within sectors, general obligation (GO) bonds outperformed revenue bonds slightly. Hospital, housing, and education sub-sectors were strong performers during the quarter, while IDR, tobacco, and special tax sub-sectors were generally weaker performers. Municipal credit spreads widened across the quality spectrum in 4Q25. The Bloomberg Municipal High-yield Index returned +1.15% over the quarter, underperforming the investment-grade Bloomberg Municipal index, which returned +1.56%. The Bloomberg Taxable Municipal Index returned +1.37% during the fourth quarter, outperformed the Bloomberg US Treasury Index return of +0.90% and the Bloomberg U.S. Corporate Index return of +0.84%.

Performance Review

The Fund’s long duration stance and decisions to overweight 10- to 30-year part of the curve contributed to positive outperformance with yields on the long end rallying during the quarter. Security selection and sector allocation were muted for the period.

Market Outlook

Municipal bonds finished the year strongly in 4Q25 with positive returns that outpaced treasuries while the bond market overall delivered the best annual returns since 2020. A catalyst was the US Federal Reserve’s (the “Fed”) dovish rate cuts of 25 bp each during both October and November. Annual supply set a record near $580 billion, up 14% year over year. Looking ahead, 2026 is expected to be another heavy supply year driven by growing needs to fund aging infrastructure, higher cost of capital expenditure, and waning of pandemic-aid. Municipal tax-equivalent yields for top tax rate investors continue to look attractive versus high-grade fixed income alternatives. This should continue to fuel solid demand from individual investors via mutual fund and ETF flows as well as direct purchasing support separately managed account growth.

We believe municipal credit conditions should continue to remain stable, supported by very high reserves and cash balances that have accumulated in recent years and modest economic growth supporting tax revenues. Many segments of the market are facing credit headwinds as we head into 2026. Tariffs, federal job cuts, and the mass deportation of illegal immigrants are likely to have negative impacts on economic growth, which could lead to some softness in income and sales tax receipts. At the same time, cuts in federal spending in areas such as Medicaid, “green” infrastructure initiatives, and federal disaster relief could pressure state/local budgets. The likely expiration of healthcare subsidies is a credit negative for hospitals, especially those operating in rural areas, while tuition affordability concerns combined with weak demographic trends are pressuring smaller colleges with weak endowments.

From a sector standpoint, we maintain core positions in state and local general obligation, utilities (water and power), public higher education, local housing, and transportation credits, which benefit from essential service demand, broad and reliable revenue streams, and in many cases explicit or implicit government support. We remain cautious on hospital bonds amid margin pressures and anticipated Medicaid cuts, with a tilt toward larger healthcare systems with dominant positions in growing markets, strong balance sheets, and those less reliant on government payors.

Although federal policy shifts under the current administration have introduced some uncertainty for municipal credit, most sectors appear to be positioned to absorb potential pressure. That said, major rating agencies have assigned negative outlooks for 2026 to select sectors, notably higher education and seaports. We recommend a more selective, risk aware approach—prioritizing balance sheet strength, liquidity, and operating flexibility. While mainland hurricane activity was absent in 2025, integrating climate risk into investment decisions remains essential.

Our duration stance is neutral-to-modestly long versus our benchmarks. During 2025, bonds maturing 15 years and beyond underperformed shorter maturities, steepening the yield curve and creating compelling value out toward the long end. We believe this segment can produce attractive relative returns as demand is likely to pick up under a more accommodative Federal Reserve. A barbell curve posture may be favorable with exposure to short maturities, which should benefit from further Fed easing while longer maturity bonds offer attractive yield in a relatively steep curve environment.

Investors should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a mutual fund carefully before investing. To obtain a prospectus, or a summary prospectus, if available, that contains this and other information about a fund, contact your financial professional or visit bny.com/investments. Read the prospectus carefully before investing. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Risks

Bonds are subject to interest-rate, credit, liquidity, call and market risks, to varying degrees. Generally, all other factors being equal, bond prices are inversely related to interest-rate changes and rate increases can cause price declines.

Municipal income may be subject to state and local taxes. Some income may be subject to the federal alternative minimum tax for certain investors. U.S. Treasuries while the U.S. Government guarantees the timely payment of principal and interest on its securities, portfolios that invest in such securities are not guaranteed and will fluctuate in value. Capital gains, if any, are taxable. Portfolio composition is subject to change at any time.

Asset allocation and diversification cannot ensure a profit or protect against loss in declining markets.

The Bloomberg US Municipal Bond Index is a widely accepted, unmanaged total return performance benchmark for the long-term, investment-grade, tax-exempt bond market. Reflects investments of dividends and, where applicable, capital gain distributions. The Bloomberg US Treasury Index measures US dollar-denominated, fixed-rate, nominal debt issued by the US Treasury. Treasury bills are excluded by the maturity constraint but are part of a separate Short Treasury Index. The Bloomberg U.S. Corporate Index measures the performance of the investment-grade, fixed-rate, taxable corporate bond market within the United States. The Bloomberg Municipal Managed Money Long Index is a rules-based, market-value-weighted index that measures the performance of publicly traded, USD-denominated, long-term, tax-exempt municipal bonds, including general obligation bonds, revenue bonds, insured bonds, and pre-refunded bonds. The Bloomberg 3-Year Municipal Bond Index is a benchmark that tracks the performance of a specific segment of the municipal bond market. It includes fixed-rate, investment-grade, tax-exempt bonds with remaining maturities between two and four years. The Bloomberg 1-10 Year Municipal Bond Index tracks the performance of investment-grade municipal bonds with maturities between 1 and 10 years. Bloomberg Municipal Bond AAA, AA, A and BBB Indices are subsets of the Bloomberg Barclays Municipal Bond Index. The Bloomberg Municipal High Yield Index is a benchmark that tracks the performance of the non-investment grade, tax-exempt municipal bond market in the United States. It includes bonds that are rated below investment grade (Ba1 or lower) or are unrated and are U.S. dollar-denominated and fixed-rate. The Bloomberg Municipal Taxable Bonds Index measures the performance of the U.S. municipal taxable investment-grade bond market. It includes bonds issued by state and local governments that are subject to federal income tax. Investors cannot invest directly in any index.

A yield curve is a line that plots the yields or interest rates of bonds that have equal credit quality but different maturity dates. The slope of the yield curve predicts the direction of interest rates and the economic expansion or contraction that could result.

Duration is a measure of the sensitivity of the price of a bond or other debt instrument to a change in interest rates. A bond's duration is easily confused with its term or time to maturity because they are both measured in years. However, a bond's term is a linear measure of the years until repayment of principal is due; it does not change with the interest rate environment. Duration, on the other hand is non-linear and accelerates as time to maturity lessens. Dovish describes a monetary policy stance that favors lower interest rates to stimulate economic growth and potentially increase employment, even if it means accepting a higher level of inflation. The Muni/Treasury ratio is a measure that compares the yield of municipal bonds to that of U.S. Treasury bonds. It's a key metric for assessing the relative value of municipal bonds, particularly for investors considering the tax benefits of munis.

Definitions

data.title: Golden Buying Opportunity: 8-9% Yields The Market Is Completely Ignoring
data.description:
One of the best ways to generate total return outperformance is to buy high-yielding dividend stocks that combine strong balance sheets with solid growth potential and favorable macro tailwinds yet are largely off the market's radar. In this article, I will detail two of these opportunities that look significantly undervalued right now and offer yields between 8% and 9%.

Hess Midstream: 8%+ Yield Backed By Surging Free Cash Flow

Hess Midstream (HESM) is a natural gas-focused midstream company (AMLP) that just delivered a solid fourth quarter. The company expects capex to decline sharply this year to the tune of 40% relative to 2025, with management guiding for even further capex declines in 2027 and 2028. This sharp decline in capex while still generating stable-to-growing volumes should lead to substantial free cash flow generation.

This, in turn, should drive continued mid-single-digit distribution per share growth each year, along with substantial excess cash flow available for stock buybacks and incremental distribution increases.

It was also reassuring to hear that for 2026, revenues are approximately 95% protected by minimum volume commitments, which provides a very stable cash flow outlook for the company.

Management expects to generate between $850 million and $900 million of adjusted free cash flow this year, which would mark a 12% year-over-year increase, with 10% annualized growth expected in the next two years. Given that the market cap is currently just $7.6 billion at the midpoint, this implies an 11.5% forward free cash flow yield that will increase further at a pretty substantial clip in the next few years, leading to a near 14% free cash flow yield on current cost in 2028.

What this means is that HESM should be able to continue paying out and growing its substantial 8.3% dividend yield at a strong pace for the foreseeable future, while also having significant incremental cash flow to use for a combination of debt reduction and share repurchases.

Reading through the lines, though, one point to highlight is that the longer-term outlook for HESM has gotten noticeably weaker under Chevron Corporation (CVX) ownership of the former Hess business, as the company has not only rolled back its capex plans but is also taking a more conservative posture towards debt, as its leverage target is no longer just 3.0 times (which is already on the conservative end of the spectrum for the sector), but expects it to actually drift lower over time, as it not only will be increasing its EBITDA, but also is going to be paying down debt with a portion of its free cash flow.

This seems to signal that they are less confident in growth or even potentially sustaining their current EBITDA once current MVC contracts expire in 2028, though this was not explicitly stated by management. What this means is that, while the buybacks and the debt reduction in the next few years will cause strong growth in per-share free cash flow and distributable cash flow, much of this accretion could be offset in the years after 2028 if CVX decides to reduce some of its production in assets that HESM services. This is the major risk facing HESM right now and is what keeps us from making it a strong buy at its current stock price.

That being said, the margin of safety is still quite large with the stock thanks to its huge yield and significant free cash flow growth outlook over the next three years that should lead to continued aggressive buybacks, helping to insulate them from potential headwinds in the further-out years. It is also important to keep in mind that natural gas volumes make up 75% of their revenues, and they also have inflation escalators on their contracts that should drive continued growth. Over the long term, the gas business should continue to provide a baseline of solid performance for the company, even if the oil business in their regions of operations continues to decline post-2028. This is because the natural gas business is more profitably positioned than the oil business is in the regions where HESM operates, and natural gas should also enjoy strong demand tailwinds in the coming years due to the AI boom. Thus, even in a worst-case scenario where CVX pulls the plug on its oil operations in HESM's area of operations, the company's profitability should still remain strong, enabling it to keep its leverage under control and supporting continued strong capital returns to shareholders.

Alliance Resource Partners: 9%+ Yield With Improving Coal Fundamentals

Alliance Resource Partners (ARLP) is an energy resources powerhouse that combines a core portfolio of coal production assets with a rapidly growing natural gas and oil royalty business. ARLP delivered solid Q4 results, as the company generated significant distributable cash flow, covering the distribution by about 1.3 times during the fourth quarter. When combined with the strong contract profile of their coal production this year, it makes the distribution look very sustainable for the foreseeable future, which is significant given that the yield is about 9.5%.

The company's balance sheet is also very strong, with a leverage ratio of just 0.56 times on a net debt-to-trailing-12-months-adjusted-EBITDA basis. Moreover, total liquidity was at $518.5 million, with $71.2 million of cash and cash equivalents on hand.

On top of their liquidity and cash, they also hold 592 Bitcoin, which, as of this writing, are worth about $41 million. They continue to add Bitcoin as they simply use energy that would have otherwise been wasted at their coal mining operations, making it an effective way of recycling that energy to create incremental value for shareholders. Note that Bitcoin is a very small portion of the company's value, so we view it as a relatively meaningless part of the business. That being said, it is an additional source of liquidity should they ever need it for any purpose.

However, the big news is the forward outlook for the company. In particular, management emphasized that demand fundamentals are improving for ARLP. They pointed out that they already have over 93% of their expected volumes in 2026 under contract with committed pricing at the midpoint of their guidance, which is a material improvement from where they were last year at this time.

Additionally, the fundamentals for the coal sector remain very strong, with management pointing out that they do not expect to see supply growth in the industry while also seeing some of the mines from their competitors actually depleting over the next few years. When combined with strong demand from data centers and support from the Donald Trump administration to keep coal power plants online, as well as Trump requiring the U.S. government to pursue long-term agreements to purchase electricity generated by coal via a recent executive order, ARLP's cash flow visibility into the future for its coal business is getting better than ever. As management stated on its latest earnings call:

Demand fundamentals continue to strengthen, supported by higher natural gas prices and low growth from data centers and U.S. manufacturing, driving increased demand for our coal supply...I don't think we're going to see supply growth. We're actually seeing some mines that will deplete over the next three years...So I do see supply pretty flat to trending down...And I believe the demand is going to go up...as demand goes up for data centers, and those data centers are completed, I expect that our energy demand for coal for data centers will, in fact, go up. So that should put us in a favorable supply/demand perspective that would support higher pricing.

This aligns closely with our investment thesis when we bought ARLP, which was that we felt the market was not giving ARLP's coal business enough value by expecting the lifetime of that business to be much shorter than it would actually turn out to be. The big reason we were bullish on coal was not only because of the Trump administration's vocal support for it but also because the realities of the AI race with China would require the U.S. to go all-in and take an all-hands-on-deck approach to power generation, which includes preserving existing coal infrastructure.

On their earnings call, ARLP management specifically mentioned the positive impact that the Trump administration has had on the coal industry:

I want to acknowledge the Trump administration's foresight in supporting policies to preserve coal units and recognize their contribution to grid reliability. From the first day President Trump was sworn into office one year ago, he understood the importance of preserving all existing base load generating units in order to protect our national security interests. Every day since, the Energy Dominance Council has worked tirelessly on this objective with particular focus on affordability, reliability, and preserving the existing coal fleet, as well as providing a regulatory framework that allows the operating lives of these plants to be extended.

Fortunately, their leadership is making a difference. According to America's Power, utilities in 19 states have reversed or delayed more than 31,000 megawatts of coal retirements based on load growth or reliability concerns, reinforcing that policies are becoming increasingly aligned with real-world grid reliability needs.

With that being said, I understand that there are sharply differing views on using coal as an energy source, as well as on President Trump and his administration, just as there are sharply differing views on renewable energy. Our focus is not to advance a certain political viewpoint on any of these things. Instead, we invest wherever we see value and opportunity and do not let political opinions impact our investing strategy. Therefore, we have invested in renewable energy stocks and have been very handsomely rewarded for doing so, and we are also investing in oil, gas, and coal stocks and have earned very strong returns on those as well.

Meanwhile, ARLP continues to invest aggressively in its oil and gas royalties business, which drove strong growth in that segment's cash flows this past year, and expects to continue doing so moving forward. This is attractive for ARLP for three reasons. One, they are achieving strong returns on these investments. Two, it is helping to grow their company's cash flows, thereby further strengthening their balance sheet and their distribution coverage. And three, it is diversifying away from coal, which will serve them well in the future when inevitably the Democrat Party retakes power in Washington, which will likely be a major headwind for the coal industry.

It is also worth noting that the recent U.S.-India trade agreement could result in increased U.S. coal exports to that energy-hungry nation, which would also be a material demand tailwind for ARLP and other coal producers.

Why This 8-9% Yield Pair Could Quietly Outperform For Years

Despite their strengths, the market appears to be overlooking these stocks, making them golden buying opportunities. HESM offers investors a juicy 8.4% NTM dividend yield and trades at a mere 6.84x EV/EBITDA, which is far below that of better-known 1099-issuing small-sized midstream natural gas-focused peer Antero Midstream (AM) (4.3% NTM dividend yield and 10.73x EV/EBITDA). Moreover, analysts expect HESM to grow its dividend per share at a 5.7% CAGR through 2028:

Meanwhile, AM is not expected to grow its dividend per share at all over that period:

Meanwhile, ARLP—due to its diversified structure between its coal mining and oil and gas royalty business—appears to be hard for the market to properly value. While it gets assigned a dirt-cheap valuation (a 5.32x EV/EBITDA and a 9.3% distribution yield) that is more appropriate for its coal business due to its uncertain long-term outlook, what is overlooked is the rapid growth in its oil and natural gas royalty business, where management is pouring its retained cash flow into growing each year. Thus, despite its sky-high yield, analysts forecast that ARLP will grow its distributable cash flow per unit at a 9.3% CAGR through 2028, giving it a very attractive combination of yield and growth that the market appears to be overlooking.

With markets increasingly rotating into real asset businesses that provide critical energy resources and infrastructure for the AI boom and also stand to hold their value well in a rapidly de-dollarizing world, HESM and ARLP are two of the most attractive and underappreciated opportunities in the market today. At High Yield Investor, we are leaning into this trend by loading up on overlooked high-yielding infrastructure, energy, and materials opportunities like HESM and ARLP. This strategy has served us very well, leading to significant total return outperformance over our five-year history while generating a 7%+ dividend yield and below-market beta.

data.title: Why Static Portfolios Fail When Risk Regimes Change
data.description:
By Bruno Buriozzi, CFA

How shifting correlations, volatility, and macro drivers undermine traditional diversification

In March 2020, diversification broke down because liquidity disappeared. In 2022, it failed because inflation overwhelmed both stocks and bonds at the same time. Yet many institutional portfolios remained anchored to static allocation frameworks that assume risk relationships will eventually revert to historical norms, even as the underlying drivers of risk changed.

This analysis examines why fixed portfolio structures struggle when regimes shift, and what portfolio managers must do differently when correlations, volatility, and macro forces no longer behave as expected. It is the first in a new series, Risk Regimes and Portfolio Resilience.

Two Crises, Different Breakdowns

March 16, 2020. The VIX hit 82.69, surpassing its 2008 crisis peak. Liquidity evaporated, correlations flipped, and diversification failed as markets moved from an initial flight to quality into widespread forced selling.

In 2022, the breakdown looked very different. Inflation, not liquidity stress, became the dominant risk. Rising rates drove stocks and bonds lower together, producing the first simultaneous calendar-year loss for both asset classes since the Bloomberg Aggregate Bond Index was created in 1980. The classic 60/40 portfolio lost 16.7%, its worst calendar-year performance in modern history.

The Question Every Portfolio Manager Should Ask

Here’s the uncomfortable truth: most institutional portfolios operate under a dangerous fiction — that risk relationships remain stable enough to justify fixed allocation frameworks. We build models assuming correlations will revert to historical means, that volatility cycles predictably, and monetary policy acts as a reliable backstop. Then reality intervenes, regimes shift, and these assumptions unravel precisely when portfolios need them most.

The question isn’t whether your portfolio can weather volatility. It’s whether it can recognize when the very nature of risk has fundamentally changed, and respond accordingly.

What Actually Changed and Why It Matters

Let’s be precise about what happened in the 2020 and 2022 regime shifts, because the details reveal why traditional approaches failed.

In March 2020, we initially saw classic flight-to-quality dynamics. The S&P 500 lost a third of its value between February 20 and March 23. Treasury yields plummeted as investors stampeded into safe havens. The 10-year yield dropped below 0.71%, an unprecedented level. For roughly two weeks, the textbook negative stock-bond correlation held. Bonds rallied as stocks cratered.

Then liquidity evaporated. Everything became a forced sale. Correlations flipped. The regime wasn’t just high volatility; it was a complete breakdown of market structure. Portfolio managers who relied on historical correlation matrices for their hedging strategies found themselves exposed on both sides.

Fast forward to 2022. A completely different regime break. This time, the enemy was inflation, the dominant macro variable for the first time in decades. The Fed’s aggressive rate hiking cycle created a synchronized selloff across asset classes. Stocks and bonds declined together for 14 consecutive months, representing 31% of trading days. The 36-month stock-bond correlation spiked to 0.66 by December 2024, compared to a 20-year average of negative 0.10.

Think about that: two profound market dislocations within 30 months, each requiring opposite defensive positioning. A portfolio optimized for the 2020 regime would have been decimated in 2022. And vice versa.

The Tradeoffs Nobody Wants to Acknowledge

This creates a genuine strategic dilemma for portfolio construction. You can’t build for both regimes simultaneously using traditional tools alone.

Option 1: Optimize for the last crisis. This is the most common institutional response. After 2008, portfolios tilted heavily toward tail-risk hedging and liquidity buffers. These positions offered little protection in 2022 when the threat wasn’t deflation and financial contagion. It was persistent inflation and rising rates.

Option 2: Stay perpetually defensive. Hold enough cash and short-duration bonds to weather any storm. But this comes at a massive opportunity cost. Over the past 20 years, equity risk premiums rewarded long-term holders handsomely. The price of permanent defensiveness is structural underperformance in non-crisis years, which are most years.

Option 3: Accept the whipsaw. Build for average conditions, acknowledge you’ll get hurt in regime shifts, and trust in mean reversion to bail you out eventually. This works until it doesn’t — typically when client redemptions or regulatory capital requirements force you to lock in losses at precisely the wrong time.

None of these are ideal responses. They’re just different ways of accepting static frameworks that can’t solve dynamic problems.

What Adaptive Portfolio Management Looks Like

The path forward requires acknowledging an uncomfortable reality: Effective risk management in modern markets demands regime-aware positioning. Not prediction recognition. The distinction matters.

Consider what you actually need to identify regime shifts as they’re happening, not six months after the damage is done:

  • Volatility isn’t a single number. Realized volatility and implied volatility can diverge dramatically during regime transitions. In early 2020, implied vol (VIX) spiked to 82 while many stocks showed relatively modest realized volatility in the weeks prior. The options market was screaming about a regime shift that backward-looking risk metrics hadn’t fully captured yet. You need frameworks that can synthesize these signals in real-time.
  • Correlations are conditional, not constant. The relationship between stocks and bonds depends entirely on whether inflation or growth uncertainty dominates. When inflation expectations are anchored and growth drives markets, you get the classic negative correlation. When inflation becomes the primary concern, correlations flip positive. Monitoring the ratio of inflation volatility to growth volatility gives you advance warning of these shifts.
  • Institutional flow matters more than most quantitative models acknowledge. In March 2020, the breakdown wasn’t just about fundamentals, it was about leveraged funds forced to deleverage, creating cascading liquidity crises. In 2022, the shift from QE to QT fundamentally altered the supply-demand dynamics for duration. Risk models that ignore these flow dynamics will consistently underestimate systemic stress.

The operational challenge is integration. Most firms run separate models for volatility forecasting, correlation estimation, fundamental analysis, and flow monitoring. Each produces valuable signals. But they rarely communicate with each other in a coherent framework.

A Framework for Thinking About Regime-Aware Positioning

What would regime-adaptive portfolio management look like in practice?

Start with regime identification that’s actually implementable. You need quantitative thresholds — not discretionary judgment calls — that trigger meaningful reassessment. Track the relationship between volatility regimes (low/medium/high), correlation regimes (negative/neutral/positive for key asset pairs), and macro regimes (growth/inflation dominance).

When these align in ways that differ from your base-case assumptions, that’s your signal. Not to panic. Not to completely restructure. But to systematically adjust your portfolio’s defensive posture and position sizing.

In practice, this might mean:

  • In stable regimes with low volatility and predictable correlations, run closer to your strategic allocation with tighter position limits. Your edge comes from fundamental analysis and security selection.
  • In transitional regimes where volatility is rising but correlations remain stable, focus on position-level risk management. Reduce concentration, tighten stops, but maintain directional exposure to fundamentally sound positions.
  • In crisis regimes where both volatility and correlations break from historical patterns, shift dramatically toward capital preservation. This is where thoughtful use of derivatives for asymmetric protection becomes essential—not to enhance returns, but to contain drawdown and preserve the ability to redeploy capital when opportunities emerge.

The Real Cost of Getting This Wrong

Here’s what makes regime-blind portfolio management so dangerous: the damage compounds.

The 2022 experience offers the clearest evidence. Portfolios that got defensive after the selloff was already underway — say, by May or June — locked in substantial losses and then missed the equity rally when inflation finally peaked. They suffered the drawdown and forfeited the recovery.

Conversely, portfolios that recognized the regime shift early — when inflation data started printing hot in late 2021 and the Fed signaled policy would tighten faster than markets expected — could reduce duration exposure and reposition before the worst damage hit.

The difference wasn’t prediction. Nobody knew exactly how bad 2022 would get. The difference was having a systematic framework to identify when your base-case regime assumptions were breaking down, and the operational capability to respond.

Where Derivatives Enter the Picture

This brings us to the natural question: if regime shifts create these fundamental challenges for portfolio construction, what role should derivatives play in addressing them?

The honest answer—the one that leads into our next discussion—is that derivatives aren’t a panacea, but they’re one of the few tools that can create genuinely asymmetric payoff profiles. When used deliberately, they offer something traditional long-only positions cannot: the ability to shape your loss distribution without proportionally limiting upside.

But—and this is critical—that only works if you approach derivatives as a defensive tool for portfolio resilience, not as a return enhancement strategy or leverage mechanism. The goal isn’t to predict regime shifts perfectly. It’s to position so that when they inevitably occur, your portfolio can absorb the shock without forcing you into pro-cyclical deleveraging.

That’s a very different conversation than most institutions are having about derivatives usage. It requires rethinking how we measure success (containment of tail risk, not P&L from the derivatives themselves) and how we integrate these positions into broader portfolio construction (as complementary protection layers, not standalone profit centers).

The Uncomfortable Conclusion

Markets will continue to shift between regimes. Volatility will spike and collapse. Correlations will flip from negative to positive and back again. Liquidity will evaporate when you need it most. These aren’t anomalies to be explained away. They’re features of modern financial markets.

The question facing every institutional portfolio manager is simple: Will you continue to build portfolios as if these regime shifts don’t happen, accepting periodic catastrophic drawdowns as the price of admission? Or will you develop the frameworks, the tools, and the institutional discipline to position adaptively?

Static allocation models gave us the 2022 disaster for 60/40 portfolios — the worst performance since 1937. They’ll give us the next disaster too; in whatever form it takes. Because markets evolve, but static frameworks don’t.

The good news: we now have decades of empirical evidence about how regimes shift, what signals matter, and what portfolio responses actually work. The tools exist. The question is whether institutions will use them.

In the next post in this series, I’ll examine how derivatives can be integrated systematically into this regime-aware framework, not as speculative bets but as deliberate tools to reshape portfolio loss distributions when traditional diversification breaks down. Because if 2020 and 2022 taught us anything, it’s that hoping your assets will diversify when you need them to isn’t a risk management strategy. It’s a prayer.
